title: CSAM/Child Pornography Online (POCSO + IT Act 67B)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM) / child pornography handled online.

Typical cyber patterns: receiving/forwarding CSAM on WhatsApp/Telegram; storing CSAM on phone/cloud; uploading to social platforms; trading links; creating or recording explicit content of a child; live-streaming abuse.

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- Information Technology Act, 2000: Section 67B (material depicting children in sexually explicit act; browsing/collecting/advertising/soliciting such material).
- POCSO Act, 2012: Sections 13–15 (using child for pornographic purposes; punishment for using/creating/distributing; punishment for storage/possession depending on intent/use).
- IPC may also apply depending on facts (e.g., criminal intimidation for coercion; extortion), but primary cyber+child content anchors remain IT Act 67B + POCSO 13–15.

Evidence checklist (practical): preserve URLs, chat logs, message IDs, usernames/handles, device screenshots with timestamps, file hashes, payment traces (UPI/crypto), and platform report confirmations.

Reporting (golden-hour): file complaint at cybercrime.gov.in and call 1930 for financial components; report content to the platform using in-app CSAM reporting; consider immediate local police report for POCSO-linked offences.

title: Online Grooming / Sexual Harassment of a Child via Chat
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Online grooming / sexual harassment of a child through DMs, chats, games, or social media.

Typical cyber patterns: adult builds trust and escalates to sexual talk, requests for images, video calls, meeting requests, or threats; use of fake profiles; targeting through gaming chats or social platforms.

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- POCSO Act, 2012: Section 11 (sexual harassment) and Section 12 (punishment), plus Section 17 (abetment) where relevant.
- IT Act, 2000: Section 67B where child sexual material is involved; Sections 66D (personation) where fake identity is used; Sections 66E/67/67A where private/obscene/sexually explicit content is created, transmitted, or threatened.

Evidence checklist: full chat export (not just screenshots), profile URLs, user IDs, phone numbers/emails used, payment requests, call logs, and any threats.

Operational steps: report the account to the platform; preserve evidence on device (avoid deleting chats); file cybercrime.gov.in complaint; seek immediate safety support for the child and avoid direct negotiation with the offender.

title: Sextortion of a Minor (Threats to Release Images)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Sextortion targeting a minor — threats to publish intimate images/videos (real or synthetic) to force money or compliance.

Typical cyber patterns: attacker obtains images via grooming, hacking, or coercion; threatens to send to family/school; demands UPI/crypto; uses multiple accounts; may create morphed/deepfake sexual images.

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- IT Act, 2000: Section 66E (privacy violation) and Sections 67/67A/67B depending on content (obscene / sexually explicit / child content).
- POCSO Act, 2012: Sections 13–15 (pornographic purposes / storage/distribution) where child sexual material is in play; Section 11/12 for harassment patterns.
- IPC: criminal intimidation (Section 503) and anonymous communications (Section 507) can be relevant.

Evidence checklist: threats and demands, UPI IDs/QRs, transaction IDs, handles, URLs, device logs, and any extortion timeline.

Urgent actions: report to 1930 for money freeze; file cybercrime.gov.in complaint; issue immediate platform takedown reports for content and accounts; avoid paying or continuing direct conversation beyond evidence preservation.

title: Recording/Sharing Sexual Content of a Child (Hidden Camera / Screen Recording)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Recording or circulating sexual content involving a child (including hidden camera, voyeur-style capture, or screen recording of private video calls).

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- IT Act, 2000: Section 66E (violation of privacy) and Section 67B (child sexual material).
- POCSO Act, 2012: Sections 13–15 (pornographic purposes / distribution / storage) depending on role and intent.

Evidence checklist: original source file where possible, device model/OS, location and timestamps, platforms where shared, group links, and witnesses.

Response steps: preserve evidence; report the content for removal; file complaint; for minors, involve guardians/caregivers and ensure victim support while avoiding re-circulation of the material during reporting.

title: Cyberstalking/Harassment of a Minor
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Cyberstalking/harassment of a minor (repeated unwanted contact, surveillance-like behavior, fake accounts, threats).

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- POCSO Act, 2012: Section 11/12 for sexual harassment patterns involving a child.
- IT Act, 2000: Section 66D where impersonation is used; Sections 66E/67/67A/67B if the harassment involves intimate/sexual content.
- IPC: Section 354D (stalking) can be relevant in parallel (fact-dependent).

Evidence checklist: all handles, message/call logs, dates/times, IP-related notices if available, and any linkage between accounts.

Action steps: lock down privacy settings, document harassment, report to platform, and file cyber complaint with consolidated evidence.

title: Fake Profile to Lure a Child (Impersonation)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Impersonation (fake profile) used to lure or target a child for sexual exploitation.

Key statutory hooks (non-exhaustive):
- IT Act, 2000: Section 66D (cheating by personation using computer resource), Section 66C (identity theft) where credentials/images are misused, and Section 67B if child sexual content is requested or exchanged.
- POCSO Act, 2012: Section 11/12 for harassment/grooming patterns; Sections 13–15 if pornographic purposes/content arise.

Evidence checklist: profile creation details, impersonated identity, chat exports, meeting requests, location-sharing prompts, and device identifiers.

Action steps: immediate platform report; preserve evidence; file cybercrime.gov.in complaint; prioritize safety planning for the child (avoid meeting; involve guardians).

title: UPI/OTP Phishing & Online Financial Fraud
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: UPI/OTP phishing, vishing, and online personation leading to unauthorized transactions.

Key statutory hooks: IT Act, 2000 Sections 66C (identity theft) and 66D (cheating by personation), with Section 43/43A as possible civil-compensation angle.

Golden-hour action: call 1930 immediately to maximize chances of lien/freeze; file cybercrime.gov.in complaint; preserve SMS/call recordings, UPI collect requests, transaction IDs, and any phishing URLs.

title: Account Takeover (Email/Social Media) & Credential Theft
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Account takeover via credential theft, SIM swap, malware, or social engineering.

Key statutory hooks: IT Act Sections 66C/66D; Section 66 (computer-related offences) may be relevant on facts.

Evidence checklist: login alerts, recovery emails, device lists, IP/session history, and attacker messages.

Action steps: recover accounts (reset passwords, rotate tokens), secure SIM, notify contacts, and file complaint if extortion or monetary loss occurred.

title: Deepfake/Synthetic Sexual Harassment (Including Against Women/Minors)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Deepfake/synthetic sexual harassment, including morphed images and impersonation videos.

Key statutory hooks: IT Act Sections 66E (privacy) and 67/67A (obscene/sexually explicit content). If the victim is a child or content depicts a child, Section 67B applies and POCSO sections may also be implicated.

Evidence checklist: original URLs, repost mirrors, hashes, and platform report tickets.

Action steps: rapid takedown reporting to intermediaries, preserve evidence, file cyber complaint.

title: Ransomware / Malware Incident (Enterprise)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Ransomware/malware incident affecting a business/organisation.

Key compliance hooks: CERT-In Directions (28 Apr 2022) — report qualifying incidents within 6 hours of noticing/being notified (as applicable).

Evidence checklist: ransom note, IOC indicators, affected systems list, backup status, and logs.

Action steps: isolate systems, preserve disk images/logs, engage IR, report to CERT-In and cyber police if required.

title: Personal Data Breach / Database Leak (DPDP)
text:
INDIA — CYBER LAW RECORD: Personal data breach/data leak (exposed database, credential dumps).

Key statutory hooks: IT Act Sections 43A (failure to protect data) and 72A (unlawful disclosure) depending on role and facts.

Operational steps: preserve breach evidence, notify affected users, rotate credentials, and follow DPDP-era breach notification obligations where applicable.
